title: European Gothic Architecture
extract: Gothic architecture represents one of the most significant architectural movements in European history, characterized by pointed arches, ribbed vaults, and flying buttresses. The style emerged in 12th-century France and spread throughout Western Europe, reaching its zenith in the construction of magnificent cathedrals. The structural innovations of Gothic engineering enabled unprecedented heights and vast expanses of stained glass, transforming interior spaces into luminous environments. Key examples include Notre-Dame de Paris, Chartres Cathedral, and Canterbury Cathedral, each demonstrating the progressive refinement of Gothic principles across different regional traditions.
